text: Hello friends, this is a case of laparoscopic myomectomy.
This patient has a large deep intramural myoma and that was completely intending into the cavity.

The surgeon utilizes a supraumbilical port for the laparoscopic myomectomy, providing a panoramic view through the telescope, which aids in the visualization during morcellation, suturing, and knotting.
So we should avoid putting the port in the umbilicus.
The surgeon performs irrigation and suction, followed by the hanging drop test to assess the pneumoperitoneum setup.
The laparoscopic view reveals two large myomas, with one deep grade two intramural myoma significantly obliterating the uterine cavity.
The surgeon introduces a ten millimeter port through the supraumbilical incision, taking care to avoid opening the uterine cavity during the procedure.
The surgical team prepares to perform the myomectomy using three laparoscopic ports, including a supraumbilical port for optimal panoramic view during the procedure.
The surgeon positions one supraumbilical port and two ipsilateral ports, arranging them in a configuration resembling a baseball diamond shape for optimal access during the laparoscopic myomectomy.

And as we know in baseball diamond concept your target you should put the index finger. And then thumb should be over the umbilicus. And one snuff box is one port. So this is the way how you will make a shape of a diamond. And two arcs are there. This is eighteen, this is twenty four. And here seven point five centimeter lateral and seven point five centimeter below. And you can put the ports. This is the second and third port.
All laparoscopic ports are positioned, including the supraumbilical and ipsilateral ports, forming a baseball diamond configuration. The surgeon prepares to start the myomectomy procedure.
The patient is positioned in a steep Trendelenburg position, allowing a clear view of the large intramural myoma within the uterine wall.

The surgeon injects vasopressin into the muscle layer of the uterus, creating transient ischemia. The diluted vasopressin solution causes the tissue to appear marble white, indicating the ischemic effect.
The surgeon begins cutting the muscle layer using a harmonic scalpel or monopolar device, creating a large incision to facilitate separation and achieve a clear surgical plane.
The surgical field shows the muscle layers with the myoma still embedded within them.
The myoma begins to protrude visibly from the surrounding muscle tissue, indicating the initial stages of its exposure during the myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon has incised the muscle layer, revealing the myoma protruding from the incision site. Another myoma is visible on top, attached to the first one.
The surgeon uses a myoma screw to manipulate the myoma, with the bladder appearing slightly distended and an indentation visible on its surface.

The surgeon attaches the myoma screw near the base of the myoma to provide proper traction. Using a pushing and pulling technique, the surgeon attempts to enucleate the myoma, ensuring symmetrical dissection around the myoma. The myoma screw is periodically rearranged to maintain effective traction.
The surgeon uses a three-port technique to optimize the view of the cavity, carefully separating the myoma while preserving the endometrial and basal layers.

So you can perform the laparoscopic myomectomy by four ports also where your assistant also can help you to do the myoma screw. But most of the time with the three port you have advantage that if you yourself are holding myoma screw then you can optimize the traction and counter traction simultaneously so both your hand will be coordinated and accordingly it is possible to give the proper direction of the traction because if the assistant is holding then both the brain two brain cannot be synchronized together because you are pulling in one direction and if they are pushing in another direction It will take more time to remove the myoma.

The surgeon holds the myoma with a myoma screw, occasionally rearranging it to optimize traction during the laparoscopic myomectomy procedure. The use of two myoma screws is suggested for better control.
The surgeon observes a bulge in the cavity, ensuring it remains intact and does not open during the procedure.
The surgeon rearranges the myoma screw near the base of the fibroid, applying traction and counter traction to facilitate the enucleation of the myoma from the uterine tissue.
The surgeon has reached the base of the uterine cavity, where two myomas are visibly attached to each other.
The surgeon carefully separates the myoma, revealing the bulge of the cavity below. The endometrial layer is visible but remains intact and unopened.

The surgeon prepares to suture the muscle layer using a number one vicryl suture with a co-op needle, ensuring the needle passes through the serosa without penetrating the cavity. The first layer involves continuous suturing to approximate the muscle, avoiding interlocking to allow for further tightening.
The surgeon takes a deep bite into the muscle layer with the suture, using the left hand to maintain traction on the suture, which also serves as a retractor in the absence of an assistant.

The surgeon passes the needle through the serosa layer and begins suturing it, maintaining a one-centimeter distance from the margin. As the suturing progresses, the previous stitches tighten, causing the margins to invert slightly.

The surgeon pulls the suture tail to tighten the inner layer further, preparing to terminate the suturing with a surgeon's knot. The procedure involves two-layer continuous suturing, with options for extra corporeal knot or baseball suture visible.

The surgeon completes the knot tying, marking the end of the suturing phase. The next step involves morselation of the fibroid, which is a slow and careful process due to the large size of the fibroid, measuring twelve centimeters.

The surgeon carefully performs morcellation, ensuring the tissue is brought towards the morcellator rather than pushing the morcellator onto the tissue. This technique helps in obtaining large strips without overshooting or causing injury to surrounding structures such as the bowel, sigmoid colon, mesentery, or omentum.

The laparoscopic view shows that approximately twenty-five percent of the fibroid has been morcellated, with seventy-five percent remaining intact. The surgeon carefully continues the morcellation process, ensuring correct port positioning and ergonomic technique.
